threats.title: Immediate Proposal Execution via Zero-Supply Auto-Success
threats.hypothesis_description: The AgentDAO state override returns Succeeded if forVotes equals getPastTotalSupply. If total supply is zero at the snapshot, any active proposal automatically succeeds and can be immediately executed, bypassing the voting period.
threats.investigation_rationale: Governance contracts must not allow vacuous truth conditions to bypass timelocks and voting periods. If a proposal can be created when supply is zero (e.g., via admin bypass or zero threshold), it can be executed instantly.
deployment_assumptions: Proposal threshold is 0, OR the ContributionNft admin creates the proposal before tokens are delegated.
preconditions: getPastTotalSupply at proposal snapshot is 0
Proposal is successfully created (Active state)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State override checks equality without ensuring supply > 0
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: May require admin to propose if threshold > 0
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Immediately after proposal creation before voting period ends

operational_questions: Can a proposal be created when total supply is zero?
Does the system rely on this auto-execute for initial setup?
validation_requirements: Test proposal creation with 0 supply
Verify if state() returns Succeeded immediately

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify or create an AgentDAO with 0 delegated supply
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Create a malicious proposal (via 0 threshold or admin bypass)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call execute() immediately as state() returns Succeeded
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Drain DAO assets or alter critical parameters
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None if threshold is 0; requires admin if threshold > 0

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete takeover of DAO execution capabilities
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Malicious proposals executed without community oversight
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - executed transactions may be irreversible

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Must execute before tokens are delegated

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Proposals executed in the same block they are created

threats.title: Unauthorized Forced Conversion of FERC20 to Agent Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The unwrapToken function in Bonding.sol is public and lacks access control. It iterates over an array of accounts, burning their FERC20 tokens and transferring agent tokens to them. Because Bonding is the owner of the FERC20 contract, the onlyOwner burnFrom call succeeds, allowing anyone to force-convert any user's tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: Token conversions should require user consent (via approvals or direct calls) or be restricted to authorized roles. Allowing anyone to burn another user's tokens violates asset custody principles.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Public function calling token.burnFrom(acc, balance) in a loop

preconditions: Token has graduated (tradingOnUniswap == true)
Target accounts hold FERC20 balances

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Public function performing state-changing operations on arbitrary user balances
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - relies on Bonding contract's ownership of FERC20

operational_questions: Is there any off-chain reason users would want to delay unwrapping?
Does forced unwrapping disrupt user tax or accounting strategies?
validation_requirements: Test calling unwrapToken as a third party for another user's address

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for a token to graduate
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Identify large holders of the FERC20 token
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call unwrapToken passing the token address and the holders' addresses
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Holders' FERC20 tokens are burned and replaced with agent tokens without their consent

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users lose control over the timing of their token conversions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - users receive the destination tokens, but timing is forced
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: User asset management and trading strategies

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: unwrapToken called by addresses not in the accounts array
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Mass unwrapping of accounts in a single transaction

threats.title: FERC20 Total Supply Accounting Failure on Burn
threats.hypothesis_description: The burnFrom and _burn functions in FERC20.sol decrease user balances but fail to decrement _totalSupply. This breaks the core ERC20 invariant that total supply equals the sum of all balances, leading to inflated supply metrics.
threats.investigation_rationale: Accurate total supply is critical for tokenomics, market cap calculations, and downstream protocol logic. In this system, the inflated supply is used during graduation to set agent token parameters, causing mismatched supply ratios.
preconditions: Tokens are burned via burnFrom or _burn

initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None for the bug itself, though burnFrom is onlyOwner

operational_questions: How does the inflated supply affect the executeBondingCurveApplication math?
Are external indexers displaying incorrect market caps?
validation_requirements: Verify totalSupply() after a burn operation
Trace the usage of tokenInfo.data.supply during graduation

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Tokens are naturally burned during unwrapping or manual admin actions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Total supply remains at initial value while circulating supply drops
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Graduation uses the inflated initial supply to configure the new agent token
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: 1:1 unwrap mapping becomes economically incorrect due to supply mismatch

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Incorrect tokenomics and market cap metrics; potential value dilution
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires upgrading the FERC20 contract or manual accounting fixes
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Graduation math, external integrations, unwrap economics

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Sum of balances strictly less than totalSupply()
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Agent token supply not matching circulating FERC20 supply

threats.title: Incorrect Slippage Calculation in AgentTax dcaSell
threats.hypothesis_description: The dcaSell function calculates minOutput based on the input amount (amountToSwap) rather than the expected output amount from the router. This assumes a 1:1 price ratio between taxToken and assetToken, which is mathematically incorrect for most pairs.
threats.investigation_rationale: Slippage protection must be based on the current market price. Using the input amount either causes all swaps to revert (if taxToken is cheaper) or provides zero MEV protection (if taxToken is more expensive), allowing sandwich attacks.
preconditions: taxToken and assetToken do not have a 1:1 price ratio
EXECUTOR_ROLE calls dcaSell

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Slippage calculated on input amount instead of oracle/router quote
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires EXECUTOR_ROLE to trigger the swap

operational_questions: What are the actual price ratios of the configured tax and asset tokens?
Are dcaSell transactions consistently reverting?
validation_requirements: Test dcaSell with tokens of different values
Simulate a sandwich attack when minOutput is artificially low

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If taxToken is highly valuable, minOutput is set extremely low relative to expected output
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute a flash-loan sandwich attack around the dcaSell transaction
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Extract maximum value from the tax swap due to lack of effective slippage protection

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of protocol tax revenue to MEV extractors, or complete failure of tax collection
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires contract upgrade to fix math
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Treasury funding and creator fee distribution

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed dcaSell transactions or swaps with massive slippage
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Treasury receiving less assetToken than expected

threats.title: Unrestricted Creation of Genesis Contracts with Privileged Factory Roles
threats.hypothesis_description: FGenesis.createGenesis is public and lacks access control. Anyone who pays the fee can deploy a new Genesis contract. The FGenesis contract automatically grants BONDING_ROLE on the AgentFactoryV3 to every newly created Genesis contract, allowing attackers to arbitrarily expand the privileged attack surface.
threats.investigation_rationale: Automatically granting privileged roles on core factory contracts to user-deployed instances is highly dangerous. If the factory has other functions protected by BONDING_ROLE, an attacker can exploit them via their controlled Genesis contract.
preconditions: Attacker has enough virtualToken to pay params.feeAmt
AgentFactoryV3 has sensitive functions gated by BONDING_ROLE

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Public function granting roles on external contracts

operational_questions: What functions in AgentFactoryV3 are protected by BONDING_ROLE?
Can a malicious Genesis contract abuse those functions?
validation_requirements: Analyze AgentFactoryV3 source code for BONDING_ROLE usage
Test deploying a Genesis contract and interacting with the factory

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker calls createGenesis, paying the required fee
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: A new Genesis contract is deployed and granted BONDING_ROLE on the factory
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker uses the Genesis contract (or exploits vulnerabilities within it) to call protected factory functions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker manipulates factory state or extracts value

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Unauthorized agent creation or bonding curve manipulation
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires revoking roles from many deployed contracts
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Agent creation and bonding curve logic

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: High volume of Genesis contract creations
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected calls to AgentFactoryV3 from unknown Genesis contracts

threats.title: Sybil Bypass of Genesis Contribution Caps
threats.hypothesis_description: The participate function in Genesis.sol enforces maxContributionVirtualAmount on a per-transaction basis rather than a cumulative per-user basis. A single user can call the function multiple times to bypass the intended contribution cap.
threats.investigation_rationale: Contribution caps are typically designed to ensure fair distribution and prevent whales from dominating an event. Enforcing it per-call defeats this purpose entirely.
preconditions: Genesis is active
maxContributionVirtualAmount is set to a restrictive value

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing cumulative state check in cap validation

operational_questions: Is the off-chain point system capable of detecting and filtering Sybil contributions?
Does the protocol intend for the cap to be per-transaction or per-user?
validation_requirements: Test calling participate multiple times from the same address
Verify if total contribution exceeds maxContributionVirtualAmount

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker identifies an attractive Genesis event with a low contribution cap
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker writes a script to call participate repeatedly in a loop
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker deposits massive amounts of VIRTUAL tokens, bypassing the cap
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker receives a disproportionate share of the agent tokens during distribution

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Unfair distribution of agent tokens, angering community
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires off-chain filtering before calling onGenesisSuccess

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple participate calls from the same address

threats.title: Division by Zero in AgentDAO getMaturity
threats.hypothesis_description: The getMaturity function in AgentDAO divides _proposalMaturities[proposalId] by forVotes. If a proposal has zero 'For' votes, this function reverts with a division by zero error. This breaks downstream integrations like ServiceNft.mint, which calls getMaturity.
threats.investigation_rationale: View functions should handle edge cases gracefully. Since proposals can potentially succeed without votes (e.g., via the zero-supply bug or admin bypass), this division by zero creates a persistent DoS vector for the contribution workflow.
preconditions: A proposal exists with forVotes == 0
ServiceNft.mint or another integration calls getMaturity

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Division operation without zero-check on denominator
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Anytime after a 0-vote proposal is created

operational_questions: Can a proposal legitimately succeed with 0 votes?
Are there other contracts that rely on getMaturity?
validation_requirements: Test calling getMaturity on a proposal with 0 votes
Verify ServiceNft.mint reverts in this scenario

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker creates a proposal that succeeds with 0 votes (exploiting the 0-supply bug)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker or system attempts to mint a ServiceNft for the proposal
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: ServiceNft.mint calls getMaturity, which reverts due to division by zero
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The contribution workflow is permanently bricked for that proposal

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inability to mint Service NFTs for certain proposals
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires contract upgrade to add zero-check

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Proposals stuck without corresponding Service NFTs

threats.title: ContributionNft Minting for Unaccepted Proposals
threats.hypothesis_description: ContributionNft.mint only verifies that the caller is the proposal proposer. It does not check if the proposal was actually accepted by the DAO. A user can propose a malicious or junk contribution and immediately mint a ContributionNft for it, polluting the contribution graph.
threats.investigation_rationale: NFTs representing contributions should only exist for valid, accepted work. Allowing minting for rejected or pending proposals degrades the integrity of the on-chain reputation and dataset tracking systems.
preconditions: Attacker has enough voting power to create a proposal (or is admin)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Minting function lacks state validation against the DAO

operational_questions: Does the frontend or backend rely on ContributionNft existence to assume acceptance?
Can junk NFTs disrupt the parent-child contribution graph?
validation_requirements: Test minting a ContributionNft for a pending or defeated proposal

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker creates a junk proposal in the AgentDAO
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Before voting even begins, attacker calls ContributionNft.mint
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The NFT is minted, permanently recording the junk contribution on-chain
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker repeats this to spam the system or create confusing parent-child links

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Pollution of the contribution graph and metadata
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - NFTs cannot be easily burned or removed

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ContributionNft mints for proposals not in Succeeded state

threats.title: Broken Bonding Launch Function via Reentrancy Guard Collision
threats.hypothesis_description: The launch() function in Bonding.sol has a nonReentrant modifier and internally calls launchFor(), which also has a nonReentrant modifier. This causes the transaction to always revert due to the reentrancy guard triggering on the internal call.
threats.investigation_rationale: Code defects that permanently break intended functionality indicate poor testing and force users to use alternative paths (launchFor) which may have different security properties (e.g., allowing arbitrary creator attribution).

proof_of_signal.pattern: launch(...) public nonReentrant calls launchFor(...) public nonReentrant

preconditions: User attempts to call launch()

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Double application of nonReentrant modifier in call chain

operational_questions: Are frontends configured to use launch() or launchFor()?
Does forcing the use of launchFor() introduce creator attribution risks?
validation_requirements: Test calling launch() to confirm it reverts
Test calling launchFor() with a third-party address as creator

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User attempts to use the standard launch() function
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Malicious actors use launchFor() to attribute tokens to arbitrary addresses, gaming creator rewards

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Broken core functionality, UX degradation, potential creator reward gaming

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Tokens launched with creators who didn't initiate the transaction

threats.title: Airdrop Calldata Parsing Vulnerability
threats.hypothesis_description: The Airdrop contract uses inline assembly to iterate over the _amounts array length, reading corresponding _recipients from calldata offsets. It does not verify that _recipients.length == _amounts.length. If _recipients is shorter, it will read adjacent calldata (like _amounts data) as recipient addresses, sending tokens to unintended destinations.
threats.investigation_rationale: Low-level assembly parsing must strictly validate array bounds. Missing length checks can lead to silent failures or misrouting of funds, especially if called by automated scripts or flawed frontends.

proof_of_signal.pattern: let sz := _amounts.length ... let recp := calldataload(add(_recipients.offset, offset)) without length check

preconditions: Caller provides mismatched arrays to airdrop()

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Assembly loop relying on one array's length to read from another

operational_questions: Are airdrops automated? Could a script bug cause mismatched arrays?
Are excess tokens recoverable if _total > sum(amounts)?
validation_requirements: Test airdrop with _recipients.length < _amounts.length
Verify where the tokens are actually sent

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Operator or user accidentally submits mismatched arrays
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Contract iterates past the end of _recipients
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Contract reads _amounts data as address data
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Tokens are sent to garbage addresses and permanently lost

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of user or protocol funds during airdrops
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - tokens sent to garbage addresses are unrecoverable

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Airdrop transactions with mismatched array lengths in calldata
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Tokens sent to addresses resembling integer amounts

threats.title: Operator Overwrite of Claimable Agent Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The onGenesisSuccess function in Genesis.sol can be called multiple times by the OPERATION_ROLE. Subsequent calls overwrite the claimableAgentTokens mapping instead of adding to it. A malicious or compromised operator can redirect users' unclaimed tokens to their own addresses.
threats.investigation_rationale: State transitions that distribute assets should be terminal or strictly additive. Allowing an operator to overwrite user claims violates the trust model that users will receive what they earned during genesis.
preconditions: Genesis has succeeded once
Users have not yet claimed their tokens
OPERATION_ROLE is compromised or acts maliciously

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing terminal state flag for success path
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between initial success and user claims

operational_questions: Is the OPERATION_ROLE a multisig or an automated backend wallet?
Is there monitoring for multiple onGenesisSuccess calls?
validation_requirements: Test calling onGenesisSuccess twice with different distribution arrays

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker waits for a successful genesis event
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Before users claim, attacker calls onGenesisSuccess again
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker sets their own addresses in the distribution array, overwriting legitimate claims

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Theft of user allocations, severe reputational damage
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - stolen tokens must be recovered or re-minted

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple onGenesisSuccess calls for the same genesisId

threats.title: Admin Drain of Unclaimed Agent Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdrawLeftAssetsAfterFinalized function allows the DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE to withdraw any ERC20 token from the Genesis contract. This includes the agentTokenAddress, allowing the admin to drain tokens that users are entitled to but have not yet claimed.
threats.investigation_rationale: Admin sweep functions must explicitly protect user funds. Allowing the admin to withdraw the exact asset that backs user claims creates a direct rug-pull vector.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(token).safeTransfer(to, amount); without checking against claimable balances

preconditions: Genesis is finalized
Users have unclaimed agent tokens
DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE is compromised or acts maliciously

operational_questions: Is the DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE strictly controlled by a timelocked multisig?
Is there a legitimate operational need to sweep agent tokens?
validation_requirements: Test withdrawing agent tokens while claims are pending
Verify claimAgentToken reverts due to insufficient balance afterward

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker waits for users to accumulate unclaimed agent tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker calls withdrawLeftAssetsAfterFinalized targeting the agent token
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker drains the contract, leaving users unable to claim

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Theft of user funds, protocol insolvency for claims
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - funds must be returned manually

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Genesis contract balance drops below total unclaimed amount
